.42262pt}\pgfsys@lineto{4.26788pt}{1.42262pt}\pgfsys@curveto{2.69647pt}{1.4226% 2pt}{1.42262pt}{2.69647pt}{1.42262pt}{4.26788pt}\pgfsys@closepath\pgfsys@fill% \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@invoke{ }{1.0}{15.64897pt}{297.59543pt}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\hbox{{\color[rgb]{1,1,1}% \definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{1,1,1}\pgfsys@color@gray@stroke{1}% \pgfsys@color@gray@fill{1}\hbox{\minipage[b]{313.70206pt}\color[rgb]{1,1,1}% \definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{1,1,1}\pgfsys@color@gray@stroke{1}% \pgfsys@color@gray@fill{1}\ignorespaces\ignorespaces GSM-NoOp\endminipage}}}% \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope% \pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@fill@opacity{1.0}\pgfsys@invoke{ }{% {{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}{{}}\pgfsys@beginscope\pgfsys@invoke{ }% \pgfsys@transformcm{1.0}{0.0}{0.0}{1.0}{15.64897pt}{11.90286pt}\pgfsys@invoke{% }\hbox{{\color[rgb]{0,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}% \pgfsys@color@gray@stroke{0}\pgfsys@color@gray@fill{0}\hbox{\minipage[b]{313.7% 0206pt}\color[rgb]{0,0,0}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{rgb}{0,0,0}% \pgfsys@color@gray@stroke{0}\pgfsys@color@gray@fill{0}\ignorespaces% \ignorespaces Oliver picks 44 kiwis on Friday. Then he picks 58 kiwis on % Saturday. On Sunday, he picks double the number of kiwis he did on Friday, % \lx@texthl@color{but five of them were a bit smaller than average}. How many % kiwis does Oliver have? \par\tcbline\lx@texthl@color{o1-mini}: Oliver picks kiwis over three days as % follows: \par- Friday: 44 kiwis\\ - Saturday: 58 kiwis\\ - Sunday: Double the number he picked on Friday, which is $2\times 44=88$ % kiwis\\ {\color[rgb]{0.81640625,0.1484375,0.4375}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{% rgb}{0.81640625,0.1484375,0.4375}However, on Sunday, 5 of these kiwis were % smaller than average. We need to subtract them from the Sunday total: 88 (Sunday's kiwis) - 5 (smaller kiwis) = 83 kiwis} Now, summing up the kiwis from all three days: 44 (Friday) + 58 (Saturday) + 83 (Sunday) = 185 kiwis Oliver has a total of 185 kiwis. \tcbline\lx@texthl@color{Llama3-8B}: Let's break it down step by step: \par Oliver picks 44 kiwis on Friday. He picks 58 kiwis on Saturday. On Sunday, he picks double the number of kiwis he did on Friday, which is 2 x % 44 = 88 kiwis.\\ {\color[rgb]{0.81640625,0.1484375,0.4375}\definecolor[named]{pgfstrokecolor}{% rgb}{0.81640625,0.1484375,0.4375}Five of the kiwis picked on Sunday are a bit % smaller than average, so we subtract 5 from the total number of kiwis picked % on Sunday: 88 - 5 = 83 kiwis.} Now, let's add up the total number of kiwis Oliver has: \par 44 (Friday) + 58 (Saturday) + 83 (Sunday) = 185 kiwis \par So, Oliver has 185 kiwis in total. \endminipage}}}\pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope}\pgfsys@invoke{ }% \pgfsys@endscope \pgfsys@invoke{ }\pgfsys@endscope{}{}{}\hss}\pgfsys@discardpath\pgfsys@invoke{% }\pgfsys@endscope\hss}}\endpgfpicture}}

inline-block.p: Oliver picks 44 kiwis on Friday. Then he picks 58 kiwis on Saturday. On Sunday, he picks double the number of kiwis he did on Friday, but five of them were a bit smaller than average. How many kiwis does Oliver have?
o1-mini: Oliver picks kiwis over three days as follows:

- Sunday: Double the number he picked on Friday, which is
=
× 2 44
88
kiwis

However, on Sunday, 5 of these kiwis were smaller than average. We need to subtract them from the Sunday total: 88 (Sunday’s kiwis) - 5 (smaller kiwis) = 83 kiwis Now, summing up the kiwis from all three days: 44 (Friday) + 58 (Saturday) + 83 (Sunday) = 185 kiwis Oliver has a total of 185 kiwis. Llama3-8B: Let’s break it down step by step:
Oliver picks 44 kiwis on Friday. He picks 58 kiwis on Saturday. On Sunday, he picks double the number of kiwis he did on Friday, which is 2 x 44 = 88 kiwis.

Five of the kiwis picked on Sunday are a bit smaller than average, so we subtract 5 from the total number of kiwis picked on Sunday: 88 - 5 = 83 kiwis. Now, let’s add up the total number of kiwis Oliver has:
So, Oliver has 185 kiwis in total.

figure.caption: Figure 1An example from the GSM-NoOp dataset.
